frameworks.name: hierarchical Bayesian learning
frameworks.one_line: Learning-rate model tracking second-order uncertainty across stable and volatile blocks.

frameworks.name: computational psychiatry
frameworks.one_line: Mapping internalizing symptom dimensions onto learning-model parameters.

frameworks.name: bifactor latent-variable model
frameworks.one_line: Splits symptom variance into general and disorder-specific factors.

core_question: Does impaired adaptation of learning rate to contingency volatility reflect a transdiagnostic internalizing deficit shared by anxiety and depression across reward and aversive outcomes?
key_assumptions: Optimal observers raise learning rate when volatility is high.
Internalizing symptoms decompose into orthogonal general and disorder-specific factors.
One learning model captures reward-gain, reward-loss, and shock domains.
manipulations: Outcome domain: shock, reward gain, reward loss.
key_findings: High common-internalizing factor scores predicted impoverished learning-rate adjustment to volatility across all outcome types.
Effect was driven by dampened learning from better-than-expected outcomes under volatility.
Neither anxiety-specific nor depression-specific factors showed this link.
interpretations: Poor adaptation to second-order uncertainty is a transdiagnostic marker, not disorder-specific.
Blunted positive-prediction-error sensitivity under volatility plausibly underlies the deficit.
limitations_noted: Two task versions ran online via Mechanical Turk.
